title: Castile & Aragon
subtitle: 1230–1492 · Union, Reconquest and the Catholic Monarchs

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From Ferdinand III to the Catholic Monarchs — the making of Spain

entries.label: Ferdinand III — the Saint who unified Castile and León
entries.hint: Ferdinand III unites the crowns of Castile and León permanently (1230) — then conquers Córdoba (1236), Jaén (1246) and Seville (1248) in rapid succession; canonised in 1671; his tomb in Seville Cathedral contains his sword and a multilingual epitaph in Latin, Castilian, Arabic and Hebrew.
entries.note: Ferdinand III's multilingual epitaph: Cathedral of Seville — the four languages symbolise his multicultural kingdom. His canonisation 1671: Vatican records. Primera Crónica General (Alfonso X, c.1270): documents his campaigns. The unification of Castile and León 1230: documented in royal charters (AHN). Linehan (1993), History and Historians of Medieval Spain: analyses the source tradition.

entries.label: Alfonso X el Sabio — the scholar king
entries.hint: Alfonso X is the most intellectually productive medieval Iberian monarch — he supervises the Siete Partidas (law code), the Cantigas de Santa María (420 songs with musical notation), astronomical tables, and a general history of Spain; his multilingual court includes Christian, Muslim and Jewish scholars.
entries.note: Alfonso X's reign: documented through his own prolific output. The Siete Partidas: multiple manuscripts; Las siete partidas del Rey don Alfonso (BnF and BNE). The Cantigas de Santa María: 4 illuminated manuscripts survive — Real Biblioteca del Monasterio de El Escorial (T.I.1 and B.I.2), BNE and BnF. His astronomical Alfonsine Tables: used in Europe until the 17th century — British Library holds early copies.

entries.label: Alfonso XI — Río Salado and the Black Death at Gibraltar
entries.hint: Alfonso XI ('the Implacable') defeats the Marinids and Nasrids at Río Salado (1340), permanently ending North African intervention; he consolidates royal power, codifies Castilian law in the Ordenamiento de Alcalá (1348 — the act that finally gives the Siete Partidas legal force); he dies of the Black Death at the Siege of Gibraltar (1350) — the only western European monarch to die of plague.
entries.note: Alfonso XI's reign: Crónica de Alfonso XI (primary). The Battle of Río Salado 30 Oct 1340: ibid. The Ordenamiento de Alcalá 1348: AHN — gives the Siete Partidas legal force. His death at Gibraltar 26 Mar 1350: Crónica de Alfonso XI — documented as the Black Death; confirmed by the timing and symptoms described. O'Callaghan (2011), The Gibraltar Crusade: the standard modern study. He is the only western European monarch documented to have died of plague.

entries.label: James I of Aragon — the Mediterranean empire
entries.hint: James I of Aragon conquers the Balearic Islands (1229) and Valencia (1238) — establishing the Crown of Aragon as a Mediterranean power; his own autobiography the Llibre dels Fets is the first royal autobiography in a European vernacular language.
entries.note: Llibre dels Fets (Book of Deeds, c.1270–1276): James I's own autobiography — oldest royal autobiography in a vernacular European language. BNE and Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó hold manuscripts. Smith & Buffery (2003), The Book of Deeds of James I of Aragon: the standard English translation. The Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean expansion: documented in treaty archives.

entries.label: Compromise of Caspe — constitutional succession in Aragon
entries.hint: When Martin I of Aragon dies childless in 1410, the Crown of Aragon faces civil war over succession — instead of fighting, representatives of Aragon, Catalonia and Valencia appoint nine arbitrators who meet at Caspe; on 28 June 1412 they select Ferdinand I of Antequera (a Castilian Trastámara) as king, bringing the Trastámara dynasty to both Castile and Aragon, directly enabling the Ferdinand/Isabella marriage of 1469.
entries.note: The Caspe proceedings 1412: Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó — the arbitrators' records. The selection of Ferdinand I: documented. Vicens Vives (1953), Juan II de Aragón: the standard study of the Caspe compromise's dynastic consequences. The direct chain: Caspe (1412) → Trastámaras in Aragon → John II → Ferdinand → marriage to Isabella (1469) → Catholic Monarchs. Without Caspe there is no Ferdinand and Isabella.

entries.label: Ferdinand and Isabella — the Catholic Monarchs
entries.hint: Ferdinand of Aragon and Isabella of Castile marry in 1469 — their union doesn't formally merge the two kingdoms but creates a personal union that acts as one; they complete the Reconquista (1492), expel the Jews (1492), and send Columbus (1492); they are the effective founders of unified Spain.
entries.note: The marriage contract 18 Oct 1469: Archivo de la Corona de Aragón — the primary document of the dynastic union. Their joint titulature ('los Reyes Católicos'): conferred by Pope Alexander VI 1496 — Vatican Archives. The Alhambra Decree (31 Mar 1492): AGS. Columbus commission: AGS Patronato Real. Edwards (2000), Ferdinand and Isabella: the standard English biography.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Final reconquest, civil wars and Aragon's Mediterranean campaigns

entries.label: Ferdinand III conquers Córdoba and Seville
entries.hint: Ferdinand III's reconquest of the Guadalquivir valley: on the night of 7/8 January 1236, a small group of Christian knights seize only the eastern suburb (Ajarquía) of Córdoba in a daring coup de main; after months of siege the main city formally surrenders and Ferdinand III enters on 29 June 1236 (the feast of Saints Peter and Paul); Seville falls after a two-year siege on 23 November 1248.
entries.note: The night raid on the Ajarquía suburb 7/8 Jan 1236: Primera Crónica General (Alfonso X). The formal fall of Córdoba 29 Jun 1236: documented in the Primera Crónica — the feast of Saints Peter and Paul. The fall of Seville 23 Nov 1248: ibid. Ferdinand III's multilingual epitaph: Cathedral of Seville. González (1980), Fernando III el Santo: the standard modern biography. The distinction between the January raid and the June formal surrender is confirmed in all primary sources.

entries.label: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa consequences — Valencia falls
entries.hint: James I of Aragon takes Valencia from its Muslim rulers in 1238 — completing the Aragonese reconquest of the eastern peninsula; Valencia becomes the most populous city of the Crown of Aragon; its Aragonese charter (Furs de Valencia) gives it distinctive legal status.
entries.note: The fall of Valencia 28 Sep 1238: documented in the Llibre dels Fets (James I's own account). The Furs de Valencia (1261): Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — the Valencian law code. The founding of the Kingdom of Valencia as distinct from Aragon: documented in the charter archives. The moriscos who remain in Valencia: documented in subsequent administrative records.

entries.label: Castilian civil war — Pedro I vs Enrique II
entries.hint: The Castilian civil war between King Pedro I ('the Cruel') and his illegitimate half-brother Enrique de Trastámara involves England (Black Prince supports Pedro), France (Du Guesclin supports Enrique) and the Crown of Aragon; Pedro is murdered at Montiel (1369); the Trastámara dynasty takes power.
entries.note: The murder of Pedro I at Montiel 23 Mar 1369: documented in multiple chronicles. The Black Prince's intervention (Battle of Nájera 1367): English chronicles document his role. Froissart's Chronicles: detailed account of the English involvement — BnF. The Trastámara dynasty's subsequent rule: documented in royal archives. Russell (1955), The English Intervention in Spain and Portugal: the standard study.

entries.label: Catalan Civil War — the crisis that forced the Ferdinand-Isabella marriage
entries.loc: Catalonia (Generalitat vs John II of Aragon)
entries.hint: John II of Aragon fights a decade-long civil war against the Catalan Generalitat — the war devastates Catalonia's economy and breaks Barcelona's mercantile dominance; John II's desperation for a Castilian alliance to end the war is the direct political driver of the Ferdinand-Isabella marriage (1469), which he arranges.
entries.note: The Catalan Civil War: Vicens Vives (1953), Juan II de Aragón — the standard study. The Generalitat's war against John II: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó. The connection to the Ferdinand-Isabella marriage: explicit in Vicens Vives — John II needed Castilian support and arranged the marriage as a strategic alliance. The Barcelona economy's collapse: documented in municipal records (Arxiu Municipal de Barcelona). Catalonia does not recover its pre-1462 commercial dominance until the 18th century.

entries.label: Castilian War of Succession — Isabella vs Joanna la Beltraneja
entries.hint: Isabella's accession in 1474 triggers a civil war — her niece Joanna ('la Beltraneja') is backed by Afonso V of Portugal; the Battle of Toro (1476) turns the conflict; the Treaty of Alcaçovas (1479) secures Isabella's crown and, crucially, divides Atlantic maritime spheres — Portugal keeps Africa and the Atlantic islands; Castile gets the Canaries; this shapes the age of exploration.
entries.note: The Battle of Toro 1 Mar 1476: documented in chronicles. Treaty of Alcaçovas 4 Sep 1479: AGS Patronato Real and Arquivo Nacional da Torre do Tombo (Lisbon) — primary texts. The Atlantic sphere division: Article 2 grants Portugal exclusive rights to navigate the African coast; Castile gets the Canaries. Suárez Fernández (1964), Política internacional de Isabel la Católica Vol.I: the standard study. The treaty directly shapes the framework within which Columbus will negotiate his 1492 commission.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: The Siete Partidas, Cortes and the development of Iberian law

entries.label: Cortes of Castile and Aragon — early representative assemblies
entries.hint: The Cortes of León (1188) is the earliest documented representative assembly in European history — meeting before the Magna Carta (1215); the Aragonese Corts is even more powerful; both assemblies develop documentary traditions of petitions and royal responses that are primary sources.
entries.note: NOTE: Cortes of León (1188) predates this folio's 1230 start — included as foundational context. UNESCO Memory of the World (2013). Cortes of León 1188: UNESCO Memory of the World (2013) — the decrees are the primary documents. The Aragonese Corts: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — extensive documentation. Valdeavellano (1968), Historia de España Vol.I: the standard survey. The León 1188 Cortes' UNESCO inscription: explicitly as 'the first representative parliament in world history'.

entries.label: Siete Partidas — composed by Alfonso X, enforced by Alfonso XI
entries.hint: Alfonso X compiles the Siete Partidas (c.1256–1265) — the most comprehensive medieval Iberian law code; but it is never promulgated in his lifetime due to fierce noble opposition favouring local fueros; it remains a theoretical compendium until 1348 when his great-grandson Alfonso XI gives it statutory force in the Ordenamiento de Alcalá.
entries.note: Siete Partidas (c.1256–1265): BNE holds the primary manuscripts. The failure to promulgate under Alfonso X: documented in the political conflict with the Castilian nobility. The Ordenamiento de Alcalá 1348 (Alfonso XI): gives the Siete Partidas legal force — text in AHN. US influence: still referenced in Louisiana courts until 1825. Burns (2001), Las Siete Partidas (Pennsylvania): the standard English translation. The gap between composition and enforcement is itself a primary fact about the limits of royal legislative power in 13th-century Castile.

entries.label: Spanish Inquisition established — 1478
entries.hint: Ferdinand and Isabella petition Pope Sixtus IV to establish a royal Inquisition — the papal bull of 1 November 1478 authorises it; the Inquisition is under royal (not papal) control; its first auto de fe is held in Seville in 1481; Tomás de Torquemada is appointed Inquisitor General in 1483.
entries.note: Papal bull Exigit sincerae devotionis (1 Nov 1478): Vatican Archives. Torquemada's appointment 1483: documented. The first auto de fe (Seville, 6 Feb 1481): documented in contemporary records. AHN Inquisición: the primary archive for all subsequent proceedings. Kamen (1997), The Spanish Inquisition: the standard modern study — covers this founding period.

entries.label: Aragonese pactismo — constitutional limits on royal power
entries.hint: The Crown of Aragon is defined by pactismo — the legal theory that royal power is strictly limited by mutual contracts sworn between the king and the regional estates (Corts) of Catalonia, Aragon and Valencia; the General Privilege of Aragon (1283) and the institution of the Justicia Mayor institutionalise this constitutional limitation; it offers a powerful alternative to Castilian absolutism.
entries.note: The General Privilege of Aragon (Privilegio General, 1283): Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó — the foundational pactismo document. The Justicia Mayor of Aragon: Archivo de la Diputación del Reino de Aragón. The Catalan Constitutions (Usatges of Barcelona, 12th c. onwards): Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó. Lalinde Abadía (1980), Los fueros de Aragón: the standard study. Elliott (2018), Scots and Catalans: places Aragonese pactismo in European comparative perspective. The contrast with Castilian absolutism: the Cortes' different powers documented comparatively in Procter (1980), Curia and Cortes in León and Castile.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Church, Inquisition and the end of religious pluralism

entries.label: Las Huelgas — royal monastery and Cistercian centre
entries.hint: Alfonso VIII founds Las Huelgas monastery in 1187 — it becomes the royal pantheon of Castile (32 kings and queens buried there), a Cistercian intellectual centre, and the holder of an extraordinary archive including the Almohad battle standard captured at Las Navas; its music manuscripts are primary sources for 13th-century polyphony.
entries.note: NOTE: Las Huelgas was founded 1187, predating this folio's 1230 start — included as essential institutional context. Extant (partially closed to public). Extant (partially closed to public). The Las Navas battle standard: in situ at Las Huelgas. The music manuscripts (Códice de Las Huelgas, c.1300): BNE — the largest collection of 13th-century polyphony in Spain. Anderson (1982), The Las Huelgas Manuscript: the standard musicological study. The royal tombs: documented in Gómez-Moreno (1946), El panteón real de Las Huelgas.

entries.label: Samuel Halevi Abulafia — treasurer, synagogue builder
entries.hint: Samuel Halevi Abulafia, treasurer of Pedro I of Castile, builds the El Tránsito synagogue in Toledo (c.1357) — the finest medieval synagogue in Spain; its Hebrew inscriptions praise both God and Pedro I; its survival after the 1391 pogroms and the 1492 expulsion is itself a primary source.
entries.note: El Tránsito synagogue (c.1357): extant, Toledo (Museo Sefardí). The Hebrew inscriptions: translated and documented. Samuel Halevi's subsequent arrest and execution by Pedro I (c.1360): documented in chronicles. Baer (1961), A History of the Jews in Christian Spain Vol.II. The synagogue's conversion to a church after 1492 and its survival: documented. The inscription praising Pedro I alongside God: primary evidence for Jewish-Christian political collaboration.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Gothic cathedrals, the Cantigas and Mudéjar art

entries.label: Gothic cathedrals — Burgos, Toledo and León
entries.hint: The great Gothic cathedrals of Burgos, Toledo and León — all begun in the 13th century under French influence — are the monumental primary sources of medieval Spanish religious expression; León's cathedral (c.1,800 sq metres of stained glass) is called 'the house of light'; Burgos and Toledo develop into the hyper-ornate Isabelline Gothic by the late 15th century.
entries.note: Burgos Cathedral (begun 1221): UNESCO 1984 — building accounts in Archivo de la Catedral de Burgos. Toledo Cathedral (begun 1226): extant — Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo. León Cathedral (begun c.1205): extant — c.1,800 sq metres of medieval stained glass, the largest concentration in a Romanesque/Gothic structure in Europe. Williams (1977), A Spanish Romanesque: contextualises the transition. The Isabelline Gothic (Flamboyant-Mudéjar fusion): San Juan de los Reyes, Toledo (1476–1504) — commissioned by the Catholic Monarchs, extant.

entries.label: Mudéjar architecture — Muslim craftsmen for Christian patrons
entries.hint: Mudéjar architecture is produced by Muslim craftsmen working for Christian and Jewish patrons — it fuses Islamic geometric decoration, horseshoe arches and brick construction with Gothic and Romanesque structural forms; it is uniquely Iberian, found nowhere else.
entries.note: UNESCO World Heritage: Mudéjar architecture of Aragon (1986, extended 2001) — towers of Teruel and Zaragoza. The Seville Alcázar (14th c.): extant (UNESCO) — built by Pedro I using Nasrid craftsmen from Granada; the primary monument. The Sinagoga del Tránsito, Toledo: also Mudéjar. Dodds (1990), Architecture and Ideology: the standard study of Mudéjar's cross-cultural meaning.

entries.label: Cantigas de Santa María — Alfonso X's illustrated songbook
entries.hint: The Cantigas de Santa María is 420 poems with musical notation — the largest collection of medieval monophonic songs from any single source; four illuminated manuscripts survive with miniatures documenting 13th-century daily life; the musical notation is the primary source for Iberian medieval music.
entries.note: Four manuscripts: El Escorial T.I.1 (the Florence codex), El Escorial B.I.2, BNE 10069, BnF Esp.361. Katz & Keller (1987), Studies on the Cantigas de Santa María: the standard scholarly collection. The miniatures as social history: Ferreira (2019) uses them as primary sources for musical instruments, clothing and daily life. The musical notation: enables modern performance — documented in recordings.

entries.label: The Llibre Vermell de Montserrat — pilgrimage songs (c.1399)
entries.hint: The Llibre Vermell (Red Book) of Montserrat contains 10 songs for pilgrims to sing or dance at the shrine — the earliest documented use of dancing in a Christian devotional context; the manuscript also contains plague songs written during the Black Death epidemics; it is the oldest extant Catalan music manuscript.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Llibre Vermell de Montserrat (c.1399): Arxiu de l'Abadia de Montserrat. The 10 songs include polyphonic and monophonic pieces. Gómez (1990), La música medieval en España: the standard study. The dancing at shrines: the text explicitly describes pilgrims dancing in the nave — 'because the pilgrims are accustomed to watch at night in the church'. The plague songs: composed c.1348–1350 — documented by internal references.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Alfonso X's scriptorium and the intellectual synthesis

entries.label: Ramon Llull — missionary philosophy and the Art
entries.hint: Ramon Llull is the first European philosopher to write systematic philosophy in a vernacular language (Catalan) — his Ars Magna proposes a logical method for generating theological arguments; he learns Arabic and makes missionary journeys to North Africa; the tradition that he was stoned to death in Tunis is a later hagiographical legend; most scholars conclude he died of old age at sea or in Mallorca c.1315–1316.
entries.note: Ars Magna (c.1274): Latin and Catalan manuscripts — BnF and Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó. Llull's Catalan works: the first philosophical corpus in Catalan. Bonner (2007), The Art and Logic of Ramon Llull: the standard English study. His death: most critical scholarship concludes he died at sea or in Mallorca c.1315–1316 of natural causes; the stoning-at-Tunis story develops in later Franciscan hagiography. His influence on later logic: Leibniz credited him — documented.

entries.label: Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada — De Rebus Hispaniae
entries.hint: Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada's De Rebus Hispaniae (On the Affairs of Spain, c.1243) is the first comprehensive history of the Iberian Peninsula — he was present at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212) and his account of it is an eyewitness document; the work defines the Reconquista as a providential national narrative.
entries.note: De Rebus Hispaniae (c.1243): manuscripts in multiple archives; printed edition Lorenzana (1793). Rodrigo's eyewitness account of Las Navas de Tolosa: the most detailed contemporary narrative. Linehan (1993), History and the Historians of Medieval Spain: the standard critical study of the source tradition. The work's 'national narrative' framing: Linehan argues it retrospectively constructs a Spanish identity.

entries.label: Alfonsine Tables — astronomy in three languages
entries.hint: Alfonso X commissions revised astronomical tables — the Alfonsine Tables — from a team of Jewish and Muslim astronomers working in Toledo; compiled in Arabic, translated to Castilian, later to Latin; used by European astronomers including Copernicus until the 17th century.
entries.note: Alfonsine Tables (c.1270): compiled by Isaac ibn Sid and Judah ben Moses ha-Cohen. The Paris edition 1483: the standard Latin version. Copernicus used them: documented in his notes. Chabás & Goldstein (2003), The Alfonsine Tables of Toledo: the standard modern study. The collaboration of Jewish and Muslim astronomers under a Christian king: a primary example of convivencia in intellectual production.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Wool, silver and the economy of medieval Castile

entries.label: The Mesta — Castilian wool and the transhumance economy
entries.hint: Alfonso X charters the Mesta (shepherds' guild) in 1273 — the guild organises the seasonal migration of up to 2 million merino sheep; but the Crown grants the Mesta immense grazing monopolies and transit rights (cañadas reales) over farmland in exchange for immediate royal loans; this stifles food agriculture, creates chronic grain deficits in Castile, and lays the structural seeds of long-term agricultural stagnation.
entries.note: Mesta charter 1273: AGS and AHN. Klein (1920), The Mesta: A Study in Spanish Economic History 1273–1836: the standard study. The merino sheep numbers: documented in 14th-c. fiscal records. The Castile-Flanders wool trade: documented in Bruges merchant archives and Castilian royal customs records. The Mesta's long-term impact on Castilian agriculture: documented in subsequent land-use surveys. The negative fiscal consequences: Klein (1920), The Mesta ch.5 — documents how the cañadas reales legally overrode enclosed farmland. The Castilian grain deficit: documented in 15th-century price series. The immediate royal loans in exchange for monopoly rights: AGS Contadurías. The connection to Spain's later agricultural problems: Ringrose (1983), Madrid and the Spanish Economy 1560–1850 traces the Mesta's long shadow.

entries.label: Black Death in Iberia — 1348 and the Jewish pogroms
entries.loc: Catalonia; Castile; Aragon (arrival via Mediterranean ports)
entries.hint: The Black Death reaches Iberia via Catalan ports in 1348 — killing an estimated 30–40% of the population; Jews are blamed for poisoning wells and massacred (Tàrrega 1348; Barcelona 1348); the pogroms of 1348 foreshadow and directly cause the larger pogroms of 1391.
entries.note: The Tàrrega pogrom 1348: documented in contemporary municipal records and now genetically confirmed — MDPI study (March 2026) using DNA from six mass graves at the Roquetes necropolis, Tàrrega. The Barcelona pogrom 1348: documented in municipal archives (Arxiu Municipal de Barcelona). Nirenberg (1996), Communities of Violence: the standard study of 14th-century Iberian pogroms. The Black Death mortality: Benedictow (2004), The Black Death 1346–1353, estimates 30–40% for Iberia.

entries.label: Consolat de Mar — the founding code of Mediterranean maritime law
entries.hint: The Consolat de Mar (Consulate of the Sea) is the pioneering maritime law code compiled in Barcelona and Valencia — governing merchant shipping, naval commerce and trade disputes across the entire Mediterranean basin; adopted by Genoa, Venice and later all European maritime powers; the most influential medieval legal exchange in European commercial history.
entries.note: Consolat de Mar: earliest dated manuscript c.1282 (Valencia); compiled versions from Barcelona. Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó holds the primary manuscripts. First printed edition Valencia 1494: BNE. Pene Vidari (1979), Il Consolato del Mare: the standard modern study. The code's Mediterranean adoption: documented in Genoese, Venetian and Pisan merchant archives. The code's influence: still cited in Spanish and Italian maritime law into the 19th century — documented.

entries.label: Consolat de Mar — Barcelona's pioneering maritime law code
entries.hint: The Consolat de Mar (Consulate of the Sea) is the most influential medieval maritime law code in the Western world — compiled in Barcelona and Valencia, it governs international merchant shipping, naval commerce, insurance and trade disputes across the entire Mediterranean basin; adopted voluntarily by merchant communities from Genoa to Ragusa.
entries.note: The Consolat de Mar (c.1258–1350): BNE and Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó. First printed edition: Barcelona 1494. English translation: Twiss (1874), The Black Book of the Admiralty Vol.III. Ashburner (1909), The Rhodian Sea-Law: places it in the ancient maritime law tradition. The voluntary adoption across the Mediterranean: documented in merchant guild records from Genoa (Archivio di Stato) to Venice. Its influence on modern maritime law: documented in legal history.

entries.label: Iberian-Genoese alliance — trade and Columbus's context
entries.hint: Genoese merchants are embedded in Castilian and Aragonese trade networks throughout the 14th and 15th centuries — providing credit, maritime expertise and commercial connections; Christopher Columbus is a Genoese working for Castile; his voyage is the product of this long Ibero-Genoese commercial partnership.
entries.note: Genoese merchant records in Seville: Archivo Histórico Provincial de Sevilla. The Genoese colony in Seville: documented from the 13th century. Columbus's Genoese origins: documented in notarial records from Genoa. Phillips (1988), Columbus: the standard biography, documents the Genoese-Castilian commercial world. The connection between the Genoese network and the 1492 voyage: Fernández-Armesto (1991), Columbus.

entries.label: Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean empire — the Catalan Atlas (1375)
entries.hint: The Catalan Atlas (1375) made by Abraham Cresques in Mallorca is the most detailed world map of the 14th century — showing the Silk Road, West African gold kingdoms, and the Indian Ocean; it is the product of the Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean mercantile intelligence.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Catalan Atlas (1375): BnF (Manuscrit espagnol 30) — digitised. Made by Abraham Cresques, a Jewish cartographer in Mallorca. The detail of West Africa (including Mali's gold): drawn from traveller accounts. Pujades (2007), Les cartes portolanes: the standard study. The Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean trade documentation: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — extensive merchant records.

entries.label: Conquest of the Canary Islands — Spain's colonial blueprint
entries.loc: Canary Islands (Gran Canaria; Tenerife; La Palma)
entries.hint: Castile conquers the indigenous Guanches of the Canary Islands between 1402 and 1496 — the methods of conquest, systematic subjugation, forced conversion and land division are the direct administrative and military blueprint applied to the Americas from 1492 onwards; the Canaries are Spain's first colonial experiment.
entries.note: The Canary conquest: begun by Jean de Béthencourt for Castile 1402 — documented. The later royal campaigns (Gran Canaria 1478–1483; La Palma 1492–1493; Tenerife 1494–1496): AGS. The Guanche extermination and assimilation: documented in colonial records (AGS and Archivo Municipal de Las Palmas). Fernández-Armesto (1982), The Canary Islands after the Conquest: the standard study. The blueprint argument: Verlinden (1970), The Beginnings of Modern Colonization: explicitly connects Canary methods to American practice.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: The Catholic Monarchs and the birth of the global world

entries.label: The Catalan-Aragonese empire — Sicily, Naples, Athens
entries.loc: Sicily; Sardinia; Naples; Duchy of Athens (Greece)
entries.hint: The Crown of Aragon builds a vast Mediterranean maritime empire — Sicily (1282, via the Sicilian Vespers), Sardinia (1323), Naples (1442); the mercenary Almogavar army seizes the Duchy of Athens and Neopatria in Greece (1311–1388); at its peak Aragon controls the western and central Mediterranean, fundamentally altering the balance of power in the eastern Mediterranean.
entries.note: The Sicilian Vespers (30 Mar 1282): documented in multiple contemporary chronicles. The Almogavar conquest of Athens: Chronicle of Ramon Muntaner (primary — Muntaner was present). The Duchy of Athens under Catalan rule 1311–1388: documented in the Archive of the Crown of Aragon. Setton (1948), Catalan Domination of Athens: the standard study. Hillgarth (1976), The Spanish Kingdoms 1250–1516 Vol.I: the standard English survey. The Almogavar mercenaries: documented as the most feared military force in early 14th-century Europe.

entries.label: Almogavars and the Catalan Company — Athens and beyond
entries.hint: After the Sicilian wars, the Catalan Company (almogavar mercenaries) takes service with Byzantium, turns on their employers, conquers the Duchy of Athens (1311) and Neopatria; the Crown of Aragon rules duchies in Greece for 77 years — the most extraordinary geopolitical overreach of medieval Iberia, fundamentally altering the eastern Mediterranean balance of power.
entries.note: Ramon Muntaner, Crònica (c.1325): primary source — eyewitness account of the Catalan Company by a participant. The Battle of the Cephissus 15 Mar 1311: documented in Muntaner and in Byzantine sources. The Duchy of Athens under Aragonese rule 1311–1388: Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó — administrative records. Setton (1948), Catalan Domination of Athens: the standard study. The almogavars: professional light infantry, documented in Muntaner and in military records.

entries.label: 1492 — the year Spain makes the modern world
entries.loc: Granada; Palos de Moguer; Atlantic Ocean
entries.hint: Three events in 1492 authorised by Ferdinand and Isabella from the Alhambra: Granada falls (2 January), Jews expelled (31 March), Columbus departs (3 August); the same year Nebrija publishes the first Castilian grammar — the tools of empire, religious homogeneity and overseas expansion all coincide.
entries.note: Granada's fall 2 Jan 1492: documented. Alhambra Decree 31 Mar 1492: AGS. Columbus's departure 3 Aug 1492: his journal (BNF). Nebrija's Gramática castellana (1492): BNE — first grammar of a modern European vernacular language. Elliot (2002), Imperial Spain 1469–1716: the standard modern study of the Catholic Monarchs' legacy. The connection between language standardisation and empire: Nebrija's preface explicitly argues grammar is the 'companion of empire'.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: African slavery in medieval Iberia — the Valencian slave market
entries.hint: The Crown of Aragon operates slave markets in Valencia and Barcelona from the 14th century — trading primarily in sub-Saharan Africans, Moors, and enslaved people from the Black Sea region; the Valencian notarial records are among the best-documented medieval European slave trade archives.
entries.note: Valencian notarial records documenting slave sales: Arxiu del Regne de València — published in Verlinden (1955), L'Esclavage dans l'Europe médiévale Vol.I. The Barcelona slave market: documented in Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó. Blumenthal (2009), Enemies and Familiars: Slavery and Mastery in Fifteenth-Century Valencia: the standard modern study using the notarial records. The scale: Blumenthal documents 10,000+ slave sales in Valencia alone 1375–1425.

entries.label: The conversos — forced converts and their impossible position
entries.hint: The conversos (Jewish converts to Christianity, mostly forced) are the central social problem of 15th-century Castile — too Christian to be Jewish, too recently Jewish to be trusted as Christians; the Inquisition is created specifically to investigate their sincerity; the blood purity statutes (limpieza de sangre) later bar them from office.
entries.note: The converso population: Beinart (2002) estimates 100,000+ forced conversions in 1391. The Inquisition's primary targets: AHN Inquisición records show conversos as the dominant category in 15th-c. proceedings. The Toledo blood purity statute (1449): the first such statute — documented in AHN (see Visigothic Spain folio connection). Netanyahu (1995), The Origins of the Inquisition: the foundational study of the converso problem. This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT: the creation of a new intermediate category (converso) that satisfies neither Christian nor Jewish frameworks.

entries.label: Pogroms of 1391 — the turning point for Iberian Jews
entries.hint: A wave of pogroms sweeps the Christian kingdoms of the Iberian Peninsula in 1391 — beginning in Seville in June and spreading across Castile and Aragon; these cities had been under Christian rule for over a century; thousands killed, many more forced to convert; the converso class created directly causes the Inquisition's founding and the 1492 expulsion.
entries.note: The Seville pogrom June 1391: documented in municipal records (Archivo Municipal de Sevilla). All cities affected (Seville, Córdoba, Toledo, Valencia, Barcelona) were under Christian rule — this entry belongs in the Christian kingdoms folio, not al-Andalus. Baer (1961), A History of the Jews in Christian Spain Vol.II. The Tàrrega 1348 mass graves: MDPI genetic study (March 2026). The forced conversions' scale: Beinart (2002) estimates 100,000+ converted in 1391.

entries.label: Remensas peasants — the largest successful peasant revolt in late medieval Europe
entries.hint: Catalan peasants (pagesos de remença) live under the 'Bad Customs' (mals usos) — brutal feudal abuses binding them to the land, taxing their marriages, and permitting physical punishment; their Remensa Wars (1462–1486) are the largest successful peasant rebellion in late medieval Europe; Ferdinand II abolishes the Bad Customs in the Sentencia Arbitral de Guadalupe (1486).
entries.note: The mals usos documentation: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — the six Bad Customs listed in contemporary documents. The Remensa Wars: Vicens Vives (1945), El gran sindicat remença: the standard study. The Sentencia Arbitral de Guadalupe 21 Apr 1486: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — Ferdinand II's arbitration abolishing the mals usos. Freedman (1991), The Origins of Peasant Servitude in Medieval Catalonia: the standard English study. This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT: the legal abolition of inherited servitude for Catalan peasants — the only such wholesale peasant liberation in 15th-century Iberia.

entries.label: Isabella I — queen regnant and architect of Spain
entries.hint: Isabella I of Castile is crowned in Segovia on 13 December 1474 — ruling in her own right; she initiates the Inquisition, completes the Reconquista, expels the Jews, and authorises Columbus; she is the most consequential woman in the founding of the modern world and the primary political actor behind every 1492 event.
entries.note: Isabella's coronation 13 Dec 1474: Segovia — documented in contemporary chronicles. Her personal correspondence: AGS — documented. Liss (1992), Isabel the Queen: the standard modern biography. The Granada capitulations and Alhambra Decree: both issued jointly but Isabella's role documented as dominant in Spanish sources. The contrast with her husband: Ferdinand handled Aragon; Isabella handled Castile — documented in their separate chancelleries.
